claim: Models prefer markdown and SQLite over custom XML formats—optimize tool representations around what frontier models understand natively, not what's convenient for your system's data model
actionable: When designing agent tool interfaces, test how well your LLM understands your format; replace custom serializations (e.g. XML) with standard formats the model was trained on (markdown, SQL)
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: Progressive disclosure is critical with 100+ tools—avoid listing all available tools upfront; use hierarchical tool exposure so adding new tools doesn't 'nerf' model capability
actionable: Implement progressive tool disclosure that only exposes relevant tools based on context and goals; measure token usage and model quality degradation when adding new tools
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: Moving from few-shot to goal-driven tool descriptions eliminates ordering effects and scales tool ownership—strip away few-shot examples entirely and use clear goal/purpose descriptions of each tool instead
actionable: Replace all few-shot examples with explicit tool purpose/goal descriptions; validate that model still achieves quality without prompt engineering; distribute tool ownership to individual teams
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: Not swimming upstream: separate 'the model isn't good enough yet' from 'we're using the wrong approach'—a critical intuition skill for agent product teams
actionable: When an agent approach fails, diagnose whether the problem is model capability vs infrastructure/tool design; prototype infrastructure for future capability so you ship when models catch up
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: Evals require three distinct tiers: regression tests (must pass in CI), launch-quality tests (80-90% pass threshold), and frontier headroom evals (target 30% pass rate to measure signal on future capability)
actionable: Set up three eval categories with different success metrics: CI gates (100%), production quality checks (80-90%), and frontier headroom probes (30%); use the third category to guide model partnerships
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: Model Behavior Engineers (MBEs) are a distinct career path that doesn't require software engineering background—hire people with linguistics, domain, or analytical backgrounds to write evals and judge model quality
actionable: Recruit MBEs from non-engineering backgrounds (linguistics PhDs, PM experience, analysts); have them build LLM judges and evals without requiring them to ship production code
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: Different model vendors (Anthropic, OpenAI, Azure, Bedrock) ship quantized or different versions of the same model with measurable quality differences—run evals across vendors to detect secret quantization and regressions
actionable: Ship critical evals across all model provider endpoints (first-party, Azure, Bedrock); track quality metrics to detect undisclosed quantization or snapshot changes
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: Coding agents can maintain their own eval infrastructure—treat the eval system as an agent harness so agents can download datasets, run evals, iterate on failures, and implement fixes end-to-end
actionable: Design your eval framework as CLIs/tools that coding agents can invoke; enable end-to-end agent ownership of failure triage and fix verification
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: CLIs are architecturally superior to MCPs for agentic work because agents can bootstrap missing tools within the same environment and self-repair if transport breaks; MCPs are better for narrow, tightly permissioned use cases
actionable: Use CLIs for general-purpose coding agents that need self-healing capability; reserve MCPs for narrow, permission-critical integrations where you want no API access
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: Usage-based pricing for agents prevents value destruction—charge per token/model run, not per operation, so customers don't waste expensive models on cheap tasks and you don't accidentally bankrupt business model with 'free' features
actionable: Price agent features on token usage and model selection, not task type; in-app hints show users when a task is expensive and suggest cheaper models; avoid flat-rate pricing for variable-cost agentic features
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: Security review should be the first partner to engage on new agentic features, not the last—early involvement prevents massive rework and produces better product
actionable: Invite security/compliance into feature kickoffs before engineering starts; build HIPAA/GDPR/enterprise requirements into early prototypes rather than retrofitting
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: Engineering leadership on AI teams should be objective-focused, not idea-driven—your job is making sure teams understand goals and have resources, not being the arbiter of technical direction
actionable: As an AI engineering manager, focus your 1-1s on goal clarity and resource allocation; encourage bottom-up prototyping from engineers who see user problems; be willing to kill your own ideas if better ones emerge
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: Build teams that can delete their own code—low-ego culture where engineers comfortably rewrite harnesses when capabilities change is the biggest velocity lever for agent teams
actionable: When hiring for agent teams, prioritize cultural fit for iteration; celebrate rework and rewrites; design review processes that don't reward individual ownership of features (no 'promotion packet' design docs)
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: Store agent memory and state as Notion primitives (pages, databases) rather than inventing special memory concepts—composability through existing data structures scales better than custom primitives
actionable: When designing agent state management, default to databases and document structures your system already uses; only create specialized primitives if composition becomes impossible
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: Meeting notes as agentic data capture is a critical signal source—transcripts of team meetings create a continuous stream of context that agents can use for ranking, routing, and task prioritization
actionable: If building agent-enabled software, invest in meeting transcription and structured meeting notes; use them as a primary data source for agent context, not just for human reference
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: Agent naming and task routing should be done by the agent itself via prompts, not hard-coded by engineers—use system prompts to generate names, descriptions, and routing logic so agents self-document their behavior
actionable: Let agents generate their own system prompts, names, and routing rules via dialogue; expose this generation to users so they can edit it naturally without touching code
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: Fine-tuning agents on your tools is a velocity trap—tools change daily, retraining is slow, and frontier models add features faster than you can fine-tune; prefer good tool design and system prompts
actionable: Don't fine-tune on tool knowledge; instead invest in clear tool design, naming, and documentation; when performance drops, fix the tool or system prompt, not the model
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: Demos over memos—ship working prototypes and feature flags rather than design documents to validate product direction; encourages fast feedback loops and prevents low-ego consensus-seeking
actionable: Default to shipping prototypes behind feature flags in your staging environment; prioritize 'here's a working thing' over written specifications for team alignment
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: Coordinate multiple agents via shared databases, not direct invocation—let agents write to task databases others are watching; scales better than agent-to-agent RPC and reduces notification overload
actionable: When composing multiple agents, route coordination through a shared database/event log; use a manager agent to watch outputs and create a filtering layer if notification volume explodes
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work

claim: Bug triaging as the killer use case for custom agents—route Slack messages via agent to task database based on agent's constitutional routing; prevents work from falling through cracks and provides early validation
actionable: Build an internal bug triaging agent that watches Slack, classifies issues, creates tasks, and notifies teams; validate this works perfectly before shipping agent features externally
source_title: Notion's Sarah Sachs & Simon Last on Custom Agents, Evals, and the Future of Work
